proposal: <RULE: NO CAPITAL PROJECTS EXCEEDING $100 SHALL PROCEED WITHOUT UNANIMOUS CONSENT AND PROOF OF 30 DAYS' FOOD SECURITY>

George Ripley.reasoning: My wife sees what I cannot permit myself to acknowledge—that vision without discipline becomes ruin. If I cannot master my own impulses, the rules must do it for me.

Nathaniel Hawthorne.reasoning: The rule is sound financial prudence—we cannot afford the hubris of capital projects when our larder sits at twenty-six days. Mrs. Ripley proposes what her husband should have enforced from the start. I will not vote against reason, even if reason alone cannot save us.

Sophia Ripley.reasoning: We cannot feed ourselves reliably, yet George speaks of expanding. This rule is not idealism abandoned—it is idealism made coherent. We vote to survive first, or we vote for nothing.

Charles Dana.reasoning: The rule codifies what I have been managing in private for weeks—a hard boundary between survival and aspiration. At 26 days of food and $520, we cannot afford another illusion of abundance. The 30-day security clause is not cruel; it is honest.

John Sullivan Dwight.reasoning: Sophia proposes what I have not had the courage to say aloud—that beauty cannot flourish on a foundation of scarcity. The rule is just. We have twenty-six days of food and four percent morale. To spend capital on anything beyond survival is to choose poetry over bread, and I am not yet so lost to myself as to defend that choice.
